coredata.dc:title: Controllable photoexfoliation of monolayer graphene quantum dots using temporally and spatially shaped femtosecond laser
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Junrui
Ma, Le
Jiang, Lan
Li, Xin
Wang, Shaoqian
Tian, Mengyao
Wang, Sumei
Zuo, Pei
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have displayed significant momentum in numerous fields due to their unique electronic properties. However, how to obtain uniformly monolayer quantum dots and explain the synthesis mechanism are still a key technical problem. Here, a rapid and controllable photoexfoliation rate of monolayer graphene quantum dots of up to 80 % was achieved by using temporally and spatially shaped femtosecond laser to ablate bulk highly oriented pyrolytic graphite targets in liquid. Theoretical calculations suggested that the temporally shaped laser can minimize the spatial range of electron excitation, maximize the Coulomb repulsion between the outer layers and Coulomb explosion within the topmost layer through multi-level electron excitation. The above multilevel photoexfoliations lead to the formation of monolayer GQDs. These findings demonstrate a perfect theoretical explanation of controllable and rapid preparation of monolayer quantum dots, accelerating its industrialization in energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Monolayer
Femtosecond laser
Coulomb repulsion
Electron excitation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation mechanism of GQDs from the bulk HOPG target. (a) The traditional Gaussian laser was transformed into temporally and spatially shaped femtosecond laser by Michelson interferometer and cylindrical lens. (b) The exfoliation mechanism for preparing monolayer GQDs using temporally shaped femtosecond laser. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Structural characterization of GQDs. (a) and (b) Representative TEM images of D-GQDs. Inset in (a): The corresponding particle size distribution. (c) HRTEM micrographs of D-GQDs. (d) AFM image of D-GQDs (Inset, corresponding height curve along the dashed line). (e) The height distributions of D-GQDs. (f) The AFM 3D height image of D-GQDs. (g–i) TEM images and i HRTEM images of S-GQDs. Inset in (g): The corresponding particle size distribution. (j) AFM image of S-GQDs. Inset in (j): Corresponding height curve along the dashed line. (k) The height distributions of S-GQDs. (l) AFM 3D height image of S-GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Chemical compositions of GQDs. (a) The XRD pattern of GQDs and original HOPG. (b) Raman spectrum of GQDs and original HOPG. (c) The intensity ratio (ID/IG) between D peak and G peak of different materials derived from (b). (d) and (e) High-resolution XPS data of C 1s of D-GQDs and S-GQDs. (f) The content percentage of sp2 C-atoms/(C–O/CO) for different samples. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The structural evolution of seven layers of graphite exposed to single pulse and double pulse femtosecond laser. (a) Time-dependence of the optical electric field characterizing the laser pulse. (b) Time evolution of the averaged height h of individual layers within a graphite slab containing 7 AB-stacked graphene monolayers. (c) Time evolution of the interlayer distances. (d) Velocity distributions with time evolution. (e) Structural snapshots of graphite surface during exposure to different laser pulses. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TDDFT simulations of the excited electrons. (a) The electron excitation process of the graphite system under the ablation of single pulse laser. (b) Local integral curve of charge density for graphite ablated by single pulse laser. (c) Charge density difference plots of the graphite ablated by single pulse laser. (d) The electron excitation process of the graphite system under the ablation of double pulse laser. (e) Local integral curve of charge density for graphite ablated by double pulse laser. (f) Charge density difference plots of the graphite ablated by double pulse laser. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Analysis of intra layer and inter layer interactions during the evolution of graphite structure. (a1) The 3D representations of the RDG isosurfaces; (a2) The 2D scatter graphs; (a3) the ELF for the initial structure. (b1) The 3D representations of the RDG isosurfaces; (b2) The 2D scatter graphs; (b3) the ELF for the graphite ablated by single pulse laser. (c1) The 3D representations of the RDG isosurfaces; (c2) The 2D scatter graphs; (c3) the ELF for the graphite ablated by double pulse femtosecond laser. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Controllable photoexfoliation of monolayer graphene quantum dots using temporally and spatially shaped femtosecond laser

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have displayed significant momentum in numerous fields due to their unique electronic properties. However, how to obtain uniformly monolayer quantum dots and explain the synthesis mechanism are still a key technical problem. Here, a rapid and controllable photoexfoliation rate of monolayer graphene quantum dots of up to 80 % was achieved by using temporally and spatially shaped femtosecond laser to ablate bulk highly oriented pyrolytic graphite targets in liquid. Theoretical calculations suggested that the temporally shaped laser can minimize the spatial range of electron excitation, maximize the Coulomb repulsion between the outer layers and Coulomb explosion within the topmost layer through multi-level electron excitation. The above multilevel photoexfoliations lead to the formation of monolayer GQDs. These findings demonstrate a perfect theoretical explanation of controllable and rapid preparation of monolayer quantum dots, accelerating its industrialization in energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Zero-dimensional quantum dots (0D) include graphene quantum dots, nano-diamonds and carbon dots, which have the characteristics of quantum confinement, unique electronic band transition, large surface area and easy functionalization [1–4]. They have broad application prospects in the fields of optoelectronics and devices, energy conversion and storage, sensing and catalysis [5–8]. Recently, GQDs have attracted much attention due to their unique physicochemical properties and wide application prospects. Specially, GQDs have advantages in excellent dispersion, highly tunable properties, ability to accommodate functional groups and dopants, non-zero band gap, and biocompatibility [9–11]. Their tunable electronic structure makes GQDs widely exploited for electronics, sensing, energy devices, catalysis, drug delivery, and bioimaging [12–15].

Generally, the direct synthesis of GQDs can be divided into two major categories: top-down and bottom-up methods. The top-down methods usually exfoliate and cut the bulk carbon precursors (graphite, graphene, CNT, and carbon fiber) into GQDs by oxidative/reductive cutting, electrochemical dissociation and so on [16–18]. The bottom-up methods normally convert the small carbon-containing precursor molecules into larger structures to form GQDs through hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, and stepwise chemical reaction approaches [19–21]. However, the above methods suffer from disadvantages including complicated fabrication processes, environmentally harmful time-consuming washing steps. Pulsed laser liquid phase exfoliation is an effective method for preparing GQDs, which is simple and environmentally friendly, and does not require steps involving strong acidic chemicals or long washing times [22–24]. Sukhyun Kan et al. synthesized GQDs from multiwalled carbon nanotube and explained the GQDs formation strongly depends on the pulse laser energy [25]. Ashwin Kumar Narasimhan utilized nanosecond pulsed laser to ablate highly oriented pyrolytic graphite (HOPG) to fabricate GQDs for long-term real-time tracking and imaging [26]. But their methods for GQDs have problems such as large sample size, random inconsistencies, and low efficiency, so it is very important to prepare uniform monolayer quantum dots quickly and controllably. Additionally, the monolayer layer exfoliating mechanism under temporally shaped femtosecond laser ablation is still unclear.

Here, we have achieved rapid and controllable preparation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with up to 80 % being monolayers by temporally and spatially shaped femtosecond laser ablation HOPG targets in liquid. The collaborative shaping of traditional Gaussian laser in both time and space domains is capable of achieving multi-level photoexfoliation of HOPG, greatly improving the accuracy and efficiency of the preparation of GQDs. Spatially shaped femtosecond laser can significantly increase the effective laser processing area per unit time. In theory, the calculation method based on time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) and molecular dynamics (MD) is used to reveal the formation mechanism of monolayer GQDs from the electronic level. The calculation results show that the temporally shaped femtosecond laser can minimize the spatial range of electron excitation, maximize the Coulomb repulsion of the outer layers and Coulomb explosion within the topmost layer through multi-level electron excitation. The theoretical calculation is well verified with the experimental results.

HOPG targets were purchased from XFNANO, Inc. (Nanjing, China). Deionized water was used as solvent. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification. The GQDs were prepared by pulsed laser irradiation of HOPG target in aqueous solution using a femtosecond pulsed laser system (Ti:sapphire, 35 fs, 800 nm, 1 kHz). Typically, before the laser ablation, the HOPG target was ultrasonically cleaned with ethanol and deionized water, and then placed at the bottom of the quartz cell for further laser processing. The quartz cell was placed on a computer‐controlled six‐axis translation stage (M‐840.5DG, PI, Inc.), and the solution was continuously moved during laser irradiation. Then the liquid phase ablation process was performed by focusing a pulsed laser on the surface of HOPG target by spatiotemporal co-shaping processing system with a cylindrical lens with a focal length of f = 50 mm for 30 min. The optimized scanning speed and interval were 200 μm s−1 and 100 μm, respectively. The total energy of the double pulse is 0.25 mJ cm−2, and the pulse delay between two sub-pulses is 10 ps. For comparison, control samples were prepared with traditional single-pulse laser, keeping the other synthetic conditions and experiment parameters unchanged. The products formed were named according to the number of pulses. For example, the GQDs prepared with femtosecond laser double pulse was labeled as D-GQDs, while the one prepared with single pulse was designated as S-GQDs. After irradiation, the as-obtained precipitate was then centrifuged and finally dried at ambient condition for further characterization. The whole processing process is carried out under the ambient conditions of normal temperature and pressure.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) measurements were carried out with a JEM-2100F field emission electron microscope operated at 200 kV. The samples were deposited on the precleaned mica plate to collect their topographic data by AFM (Bruker Multimode 8) in tapping mode. X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with Cu Kα radiation (Bruker D8 ADVANCE). Raman spectra were detected by inVia reflex (RENISHAW) under 532 nm laser excitation. The power of the excitation laser line was kept well below 1 mW to avoid damage to composites. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher) with an exciting source of Al Kα = 1486.6 eV. The binding energies obtained in the XPS spectral analysis were calibrated by C 1s as reference energy (C 1s = 284.8 eV). Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed using an F-7000 FL spectrophotometer.

In this work, all numerical calculations are performed using the real time and real space TDDFT program OCTOPUS. The surface of hexagonal graphite is simulated by an infinite planar periodic array containing 7 hexagonal (AB) stacked graphene layers, which are connected by weak van der Waals interactions. The original graphene cells were expanded by 2 × 2 in the plane of the layer, so each 3D supercell contained 56 carbon atoms. In order to reduce the interaction between adjacent simulation units, the thickness of the vacuum layer between adjacent graphene systems is set to 60 Å to minimize the coupling between them. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerh (PBE) exchange correlation functional under the generalized gradient approximation (GGA-PBE) is used in all calculations. In order to better explain the weak interlayer gravity in layered crystals, the van der Waals interaction is also considered, and the DFT-D3 method proposed by Grimme is used to correct the van der Waals interaction between graphite layers. The electron-ion interaction is processed by Norm-conserving pseudopotential. At the same time, the convergence accuracy of self-consistent field (SCF) is set to 10−7 Ha, and the Smearing value is set to 0.001 eV. The real space was sampled with a grid spacing of 0.3 a.u. in all directions of X, Y and Z.

The structural relaxation is first performed on the above geometric model to minimize the force on ions. In the process of geometric optimization, the lattice is fixed, while all atomic positions are allowed to relax. The convergence criterion for force is below 10−3 eV/Å, and the energy convergence threshold is 10−5 eV.

Based on the theoretical framework of TDDFT, the real-time simulation of laser ablation is carried out with the geometrically optimized structure. In the case of single-pulse and double-pulse femtosecond laser, the regulation of the exfoliating mechanism of two-dimensional layered materials is studied respectively. The ion motion in this study is simulated by the Ehrenfest method, which represents transitions between different potential energy surfaces within the mean field approximation. Here, for a single pulse laser electric field
A
( t )
, it can be written as
A
( t )
=
I 0
c
ω
f
( t )
[
1
1 +
ϵ 2
cos
(
ω t + ϕ
)
e ˆ
x
+
ϵ
1 +
ϵ 2
sin
(
ω t + ϕ
)
e ˆ
y
]
where
I 0
is the incident laser intensity,
f
( t )
is the (normalized) envelope,
ω
is the carrier frequency and
ϕ
is the carrier envelope phase (CEP).

In order to make the exfoliating process visible on a shorter time scale, and due to quite high computational cost, a shorter Gaussian laser pulse is considered here for a single pulse. The laser pulse duration is 10 fs (full width at half maximum, FWHM), the vector potential is a sin-square envelope, and the laser wavelength is 800 nm. In all calculations, the laser polarization is linear. The peak intensity corresponding to the laser intensity used in the experiment was adopted, that is, for single-pulse femtosecond laser, the laser intensity was set to 5.65 × 1013 W/cm2, allowing for direct comparison between experimental results and theoretical calculation results. To ensure the stability of time propagation and convergence of step size, the time step of Δt = 0.001 fs is used for propagation. In all calculations, the carrier envelope phase φ = 0 is assumed. For temporally-shaped femtosecond laser, the pulse delay between the two sub-pulses is set to 20 fs.

The preparation of monolayer GQDs was carried out by focusing the temporally and spatially shaped laser on the bulk highly ordered pyrolytic graphite targets immersed in water, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 a. The detailed preparation method can be found in Supporting Information. By designing the spatial optical field distribution of ultrafast laser, the point light spots of Gaussian beam were extended to line-like light spots, improving the effective area of laser ablation per unit time (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2). Furthermore, it is found that the femtosecond laser with time-domain shaping can realize multi-level ionization of the target, which can dynamically regulate the instantaneous local ionization electron density of bulk target and control the morphology, size and composition of the product.

Specifically, based on the subsequent experimental and theoretical calculation results, a possible exfoliation mechanism for preparing monolayer GQDs using temporally shaped femtosecond laser ablation of HOPG target in liquid is proposed, as shown in Fig. 1b. When the first sub-pulse interacts with the HOPG target in the liquid, the electrons can be ejected from HOPG via photoionization effect, which will generate different degrees of high-intensity electric field on the surface of different layers, among which the outer layer of graphite has a greater degree of ionization, and the amount of positive charge accumulation in the outer layer is also relatively large. At the same time, a very strong Coulomb repulsion will be generated between the positive charges, which weakens the van der Waals interaction between the layers of graphite (step 1 in schematic illustration). Subsequently, when the second sub-pulse is applied, the electrons in the material can be continuously excited. Similarly, the amount of electron excitation of the outer layer is relatively large, resulting in a significant increase in the density of positive charge of the outer layer. This phenomenon further increases the Coulomb repulsion between the top graphite layers, enabling the successful exfoliation single graphene nanosheets (step 2 in schematic illustration). In addition, due to the larger density of positive charge accumulated in the top layer of graphite in the case of temporally shaped laser, Coulomb explosion can also occur in this layer (step 3 in schematic illustration). The above multilevel photoionization, multilevel photoexfoliation of HOPG, Coulomb repulsion and explosion lead to the formation of monolayer GQDs.

The morphologies and size distribution of GQDs obtained at different laser processing parameters were determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM) analysis. Fig. 2 a and b show a typical TEM image of the obtained D-GQDs prepared by temporally shaped femtosecond laser, indicating the exceptionally narrow and uniform size distribution of GQDs (3.6 ± 2 nm) (The inset of Fig. 2a). In addition, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) imaging of D-GQDs (Fig. 2c) reveals clearly the high crystallinity of GQDs with an in-plane lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, which corresponds to (110) plane of graphene [27]. Also, the measurement results of AFM (Fig. 2d) also show that the products obtained by temporally shaped femtosecond laser have the characteristics of GQDs, which is consistent with the above TEM analysis results. Remarkably, results of further statistical analyses of AFM image on D-GQDs demonstrate that ∼ 80 % (Fig. 2e) of the resultant GQDs are monolayer with height of less than 0.6 nm (Fig. 2f) [28–30].

Moreover, the processing results of traditional Gaussian beam were compared. The S-GQDs prepared by single pulse displays similar morphological characteristics to D-GQDs (Fig. 2g–i), but their size distribution range is relatively wide, ranging from 2 to 30 nm (The inset of Fig. 2g). The corresponding AFM data revealed a typical topographic height of approximately 0.5–2.5 nm (Fig. 2j), implying that most of the GQDs produced consist of few or multi layers (Fig. 2l) [31–33], and the monolayer GQDs accounted for only about 3 % of the total GQDs (Fig. 2k). The above results indicate that the controllable preparation of monolayer graphene quantum dots with uniform particle size distribution can be achieved by temporally shaped femtosecond laser ablation in liquid. In order to investigate the photoluminescence properties of our GQDs, we measure the photoluminescence (PL) characteristics (Fig. S3). The PL of GQDs depends on parameters such as size, shape and surface state. PL show a single signal at 425 nm (blue emission) and Excitation has been performed at 310 nm. According to the quantum confinement effect of the conjugated π-domains in GQDs, PL energy will have a blue shift when the size of sp2 domain gets smaller. The narrow and uniform size of D-GQDs distribution (3.6 ± 2 nm) leads to the blue emission spectrum. Temperature, pH has little effect on the emission peak wavelength of D-GQDs, and these conditions relatively affect the intensity of the luminescence (Fig. S4). The D-GQDs before and after centrifugation were compared (Fig. S5), where the graphene solution before centrifugation contained nanosheets, nanoparticles, and QDs, which were darker in color than the GQD solution after centrifugation.

The composition of the products obtained under the above two processing conditions was analyzed. Fig. 3 a shows the typical XRD pattern of D-GQDs and S-GQDs, and it is compared with the original HOPG target. For the HOPG before laser processing, a series of characteristic peaks at 27°, 55° and 83° are well indexed to the (001), (002) and (003) facets of HOPG [34,35], respectively, indicating its high crystallinity. Instead, in D-GQDs, the sharp diffraction peaks corresponding to HOPG disappeared and were replaced by weak broad peaks, indicating a lack of long-range order between graphene sheets and a significant reduction of the size [27,36]. In addition, it is worth noting that the XRD patterns of S-GQDs are also similar to those of D-GQDs, but the broadening degree of the diffraction peaks is relatively weak. The above results clearly show that the size distribution of the products obtained by temporally shaped femtosecond laser is relatively uniform and the distribution range is narrow, which is consistent with the results of the above morphology analysis.

The microstructure evolution during laser ablation was further verified by Raman spectroscopy. The Raman spectra of GQDs obtained by double pulse and single pulse irradiation are compared with that of HOPG in Fig. 3b. As illustrated, for pristine HOPG, there are two dominant peaks at ∼1588 cm−1 (G-band) and ∼2713 cm−1 (2D-band) [37–39]. Compared with HOPG, both types of femtosecond laser ablation in liquid result in the appearance of D peak at ∼1338 cm−1, which can be assigned to the structural defects of the sp2 carbon and the large number of edge structures for the smaller size of GQDs [40–42]. In addition, the intensity ratio of D band to G band (ID/IG) is statistically analyzed, which is generally related to the defect density and the crystallite size [43–45]. According to Fig. 3c, the ID/IG ratio of D-GQDs is 0.8257, which is much larger than that of S-GQDs (∼0.6841). The results indicate that the GQDs with relatively small size and large number of edges can be prepared by temporally shaped femtosecond laser. Moreover, Li et al. mentioned that water molecules can be ionized by laser pulses to produce hydrogen bonds and hydrogen-oxygen bonds, and the laser processing by double pulses in solution is more conducive to the ionization of water [22], which promotes the incorporation of oxygen-containing groups into the original graphite to form structural defects, further increasing the defect density in GQDs. Thus, the temporally shaped femtosecond laser ablation is more efficient for the preparation of GQDs with smaller size and rich defects.

As shown in Fig. 3d and e, the elemental composition of D-GQDs and S-GQDs was further determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). It can be seen that the C 1s spectra of D-GQDs and S-GQDs can be further deconvoluted into three different peaks located at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV and 288 eV, corresponding to sp2 C-atoms, C–O bonds and CO bonds [46,47], respectively. Such results confirm that oxygen-containing functional groups can be introduced into the GQDs during laser ablation in liquid. In order to investigate the doping level of oxygen elements for different laser processing methods, the percentage of C–O/CO of different samples was qualitatively analyzed based on the deconvoluted peaks. As shown in Fig. 3f, the proportion of C–O/CO of D-GQDs accounts for about 43.2 %, which is higher than that of S-GQDs (29.8 %). The above results indicate that double pulse ablation has a faster water photoionization rate and higher processing efficiency, which is consistent with the X-ray diffraction (Fig. 3a) and Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 3b) analyses.

On the basis of the above experimental results, here we simulated the time evolution of the photo-exfoliation of HOPG under different laser processing conditions based on TDDFT-MD calculations, so as to reveal the exfoliating mechanism of monolayer GQDs and explain the experimental results. Fig. 4 depicts the structural evolution of seven layers of graphite exposed to single pulse and double pulse femtosecond laser. Firstly, it is noted that during the real time evolution of the geometric structure, the structure changes at the top and bottom surface of the graphite are almost the same due to the spatial symmetry of the initial geometry model, so more targeted analysis will be conducted on the structural evolution of the upper half of the graphite structure. The comparison of the time evolution of the heights of different graphite layers indicates that the outermost three layers of graphite have different degrees of Z-displacement under the two processing conditions. The height change of the outermost layer under the condition of temporally shaped laser is greater than that under the condition of single pulse, while the height change of the subsurface layer and the third layer of temporally shaped laser is relatively larger than the single pulse (Fig. 4b).

To intuitively demonstrate the structural evolution of graphite, the details of the time evolution of the interlayer spacing during laser irradiation are shown in Fig. 4c, which is defined by the distance between the centroids of the adjacent graphitic layers. Specifically, as for the processing results of the double pulse, as shown in Fig. 4b and c, it can be seen that the interlayer distance of the topmost layer is strongly expanded, which undoubtedly weakens the interlayer vdW interaction and further promotes the exfoliation of the topmost graphene monolayer. Moreover, it is observed that the structural changes of the sublayer are much smaller under the double pulse laser irradiation, and the displacement of the innermost atomic layer is almost negligible compared with that of the outermost layer, which means that the temporally shaped femtosecond laser is more conducive to the exfoliation of monolayer graphene. And the large gap between the exfoliated layers can prevent the layers from accumulating again by vdW attraction. In addition, a snapshot of the time evolution of typical lattice dynamics under double pulse laser conditions (Fig. 4e, final state-D) shows that, the exfoliated 2D graphene sheets will be fragmented into GQDs with the continuous evolution of time. And the carbon atom spacing at the fracture is 1.824 Å, which is significantly greater than the carbon atom spacing of the initial graphite structure (1.46 Å). It is generally considered that there is no chemical bond formed between atoms when the atomic spacing is larger than 1.15 times of the sum of the CSD radius of the atom (Cambridge Structural Database). Therefore, the bonding between C atoms is greatly weakened and the nanosheets are fractured under the irradiation of temporally shaped laser.

In contrast, in the simulation of graphite exfoliating by single pulse, it can be seen that in addition to the large expansion of the topmost layer, the interlayer spacing of the sublayer also increases greatly with the evolution of time, which is significantly faster than that of the double pulse laser processing (Fig. 4c). Moreover, the change of interlayer spacing of the third layer is also relatively quick compared to that of the double pulse ablation, indicating that the monolayer exfoliation efficiency of graphite is relatively low under the irradiation of single pulse laser. In addition, no fracture of the C–C bond was observed in the structural snapshot of the single pulse laser processing conditions (Fig. 4e, final state-S) at the time scale studied, that is, no GQDs were produced under this condition. The theoretical calculation results under the two processing parameters of single and double pulse laser are qualitatively consistent with the above experimental results, verifying that temporally shaped femtosecond laser can improve the processing efficiency for preparing GQDs and have higher controllability of processing results.

On the basis of the structural evolution of graphite processed by the above two conditions, we further analyze the time evolution of the motion velocity (Fig. 4d) and force (Fig. S6, Supporting Information) acting on carbon atoms in each layer of the graphite under the irradiation of laser to intuitively understand the regulation of different shapes of laser on the exfoliation process. Only the Z-axis component of the force is discussed, as the exfoliating direction is along the Z-axis. It can be seen that under the two processing conditions, the force acting on atoms in the outer layer is significantly larger than that on atoms in the inner layer. Further, as shown in Fig. S6a (Supporting Information), by comparing the forces acting on the carbon atoms in the outermost layer under the action of two laser processing conditions, it can be seen that within the time scale of the first subpulse, the forces on the carbon atom under the action of single pulse laser is significantly larger than that of the double pulses due to the high laser energy density of the single pulse. After the second sub-pulse is applied, the force on the carbon atoms under the irradiation of the double pulse increases significantly, and gradually larger than that under single pulse laser irradiation. The force on the carbon atoms in the subsurface layer (Fig. S6b, Supporting Information) and the third layer (Fig. S6c, Supporting Information) of graphite is also analyzed, and it can be seen that the force under the single pulse action is always larger than that under double pulse action during the entire simulation process. This result is consistent with the above trend of structural evolution (Fig. 4c).

Correspondingly, the velocity of C atoms in each layer is monitored as a function of time and plotted in Fig. 4d. Only the Z-axis component of velocity is discussed here. We first analyze the velocity of C atom in each layer under the condition of single pulse. It can be observed that the C atom in the outermost layer moves the fastest, and the movement speed of C atoms closer to the inner side of graphite gradually slows down. This is mainly due to the larger number of excited electrons in the outer graphite layer under the ablation of laser, which leads to a larger amount of positive charge accumulated in the layer, resulting in a greater coulomb repulsion between the outer layers and the faster the movement speed of C atom in the outer graphite layer. The motion trend of C atoms in different layers under the ablation of femtosecond laser double pulse laser is similar to that under the condition of single pulse.

In addition, in order to comprehensively compare the regulatory tendency of structure evolution by single-pulse and double-pulse laser ablation, we have separately compared the motion velocity of C atoms in each layer under the two processing conditions (Fig. S7, Supporting Information). In the outermost layer, it can be observed that the motion velocity of the C atoms in the outermost layer under the ablation of double pulse is smaller than that under the ablation of single pulse at the initial stage (the first sub-pulse), while their motion velocity ablated by double pulse increases significantly after the application of the second sub-pulse and gradually exceeds that under the condition of the single pulse. The main reason for this result is that the single pulse has a higher energy density, so the atoms in the outermost layer move faster in the initial stage. However, with the application of the second subpulse of the double pulse, the electrons in the outer layer can be excited preferentially, so that the amount of positive charge accumulated in the outer layer is more than that of the single pulse, so the coulomb repulsion is relatively larger and the atoms of the outermost layer move faster under the ablation of double pulse laser. However, the motion velocity of C atom in the subsurface layer (Fig. S7b, Supporting Information) and the third layer (Fig. S7c, Supporting Information) of graphite is relatively fast in the whole simulation stage under the ablation of single pulser laser, which is consistent with the above discussion on the time evolution of structure and atomic force. According to the above analysis, it has been confirmed that laser irradiation with appropriate parameters can significantly weaken the interlayer vdW interaction and provide additional kinetic energy for these atoms, thus contributing to interlayer dissociation.

The excitation of free electrons determines the final result of laser processing. To understand the processing mechanism of graphite exfoliating, we discussed and compared the electron excitation process of the graphite system under the two laser processing conditions in detail. As shown in Fig. 5 a, we first analyze the evolution of the electron excitation process of the graphite system under the ablation of single pulse. Due to the much higher energy density of single pulse laser, there are more photons per unit area, which is more conducive to the absorption of photons. Therefore, the number of excited electrons in the system oscillates with the oscillation of the laser waveform and the excitation rate is very fast under the ablation of laser. After that, it could be observed that the number of excited electrons continued to rise, which can be explained by the fact that the escape motion process of high-energy electrons generated by the previous photoionization could cause collision ionization, which can further ionize the graphite system, resulting in a continuous increase in the number of excited electrons. It can be seen from Fig. 5a, the total number of excited electrons at the final state of evolution is 140 under the condition of single pulse laser.

We further investigate the process of electron excitation in the case of double pulse laser ablation. As can be seen from Fig. 5d, for the temporally shaped femtosecond laser ablation, the number of excited electrons gradually oscillates with the irradiation of laser, similar to the case of single pulse irradiation. However, as the number of pulses increases, the energy density of the sub-pulse laser is smaller than that of its corresponding single pulse laser, so the number of photons per unit area is less, which results in less photon absorption in the case of double pulse laser. Thus, the increasing trend of the number of excited electrons is relatively gentle. After laser ablation, similar to the situation of the single pulse laser ablation, the number of excited electrons also continues to increase due to the collision ionization between the high-energy electrons. It can be seen from Fig. 5d that at the final state of evolution, the total number of excited electrons is 130, which is less than the number of excited electrons in the case of single pulse laser ablation.

To compare the difference of the number of ionized electrons in different layers of graphite system under the ablation of single pulse and double pulse laser, we analyze the electron density of the initial graphite structure and the excited state structure of the final state of laser ablation. Figs. S8–9 show the isosurface of charge density of the graphite structure of the evolved final state under the above two conditions. By integrating the electron density of the above structures along the axial direction, the local integral curve obtained reflects the amount of electrons on the XY cross section corresponding to different Z values. Due to the symmetrical evolution of the graphite system under laser ablation, only the top four layers of graphite structure are analyzed here. As shown in Fig. 5b, the electron density of each layer of the initial graphite structure is almost the same. From the amount of the electron of the excited state structure obtained by the single pulse laser in Fig. 5b, it can be clearly seen that compared with the initial structure, almost all layers of graphite undergo different degrees of ionization. The percentages of the number of excited electrons in the topmost layer, the subsurface layer and the third layer were 3.29 %, 2.77 % and 2.18 % (calculated by the integral area), respectively. Similarly, we have conducted a statistical analysis of the excited electrons in the structure obtained by double pulse laser ablation. It can be seen that the number of excited electrons in the topmost layer, the subsurface layer, and third layers are 6.53 %, 1.53 %, and 0.09 %, respectively (Fig. 5e). Obviously, the number of excited electrons of topmost graphite ablated by temporally shaped femtosecond laser is significantly larger than that of the single pulse case, while the number of excited electrons in the subsurface layer and the third layer is less than that of single pulse laser case. This is mainly due to the high energy density of the single pulse laser, which can simultaneously cause ionization of electrons in several layers of graphite. While for the double-pulse femtosecond laser, the second sub-pulse can continue to excite electrons in the outer layer after the first laser ablation, and the electrons of inner layer are less excited due to the lower laser energy density. Therefore, more positive charges accumulated in the layer after the excitation of electrons of the outer layers, resulting in a strong coulomb repulsion between the outer layers of graphite, which significantly reduces the interlayer interaction. These analyses confirmed that the double-pulse laser is more conducive to monolayer exfoliation, which is consistent with the above experimental results.

From the above discussion, it can be seen that laser irradiation can cause photoelectron ejection, leaving positive charges accumulated in the graphite layers, which can drive coulomb repulsion and lead to fragmentation of the exfoliated graphene layer and the formation of smaller GQDs. This effect was previously regarded as the mechanism for laser fragmentation of noble metal nanoparticles. At the same time, due to the significantly larger number of excited electrons at the topmost layer under the action of double pulse laser than that of single pulse laser, the former is more conducive to laser fragmentation of nanosheets. Therefore, it can be concluded that the electron excitation process under laser irradiation simultaneously determines the Coulomb repulsion between the layers to achieve the exfoliation of nanosheets, as well as the Coulomb explosion within the nanosheets to achieve the fragmentation of the nanosheets. Moreover, temporally shaped femtosecond laser has a higher generation rate of monolayer quantum dot.

Electron density difference (EDD) is a commonly used method to analyze electron transfer. Here, we further reveal the exfoliating mechanism of multilayer graphite under the ablation of laser through the EDD analysis. Fig. 5c and f shows the isosurface of charge density difference of graphite structure under the ablation of the double-pulse and single-pulse laser, which is obtained by subtracting the charge density of the initial graphite structure (t = 0 fs) from the charge density of the structure at the moment when the atoms in graphite are about to move (17 fs after laser ablation). Firstly, from the EDD under the condition of single pulse laser ablation (Fig. 5c), it can be seen that due to the high energy density of single pulse, the charge density of the outermost layers of graphite is easy to change, and a large number of charge clouds appear on different graphite layers, in which the blue cloud represents the accumulation of positive charge. As for the charge density difference in the case of double pulse, we find that electrons in the outer layer of graphite are preferentially excited under the ablation of double-pulse laser (Fig. 5f), so positive charges are easy to form and accumulate on the outer layer. In contrast, the charge density difference of the inner graphite is relatively small, indicating that there are less excited electrons in the inner graphite and thus less positive charge accumulated in it. It can be inferred that the electrons of the outer layer of graphite can continue to be excited preferentially after the second sub pulse ablation. However, due to the lower laser energy density, the inner electrons are also excited less, which leads to the continuous increase of the positive charge density on the outer side. When the positive charge density is large enough, the coulomb repulsion between the outermost layers will exceed the vdW interaction between the layers, resulting in layer-by-layer exfoliation from the outermost layer of the layered structure. Therefore, by comparing the charge density difference between the double pulse and single pulse laser, it can be clearly seen from the comparison of the EDD that the repulsive force between the outermost graphite layers of the double pulse is significantly larger than that under the ablation of single pulse, while the repulsion between the positive charges of the inner layer is less than that under the condition of single pulse laser ablation. This makes monolayer exfoliating easy to occur under the ablation of double pulse laser. Therefore, we conclude that the exfoliation of graphite is mainly due to the excitation of electrons in the graphite layer under laser ablation, resulting in a large number of positive charges accumulated in the layer, and its repulsion can break the weak interlayer bonding. Similarly, it is shown that temporally shaped femtosecond laser is a more efficient method for single layer exfoliating.

Investigating the interactions between graphite layers is essential for determining the exfoliation mechanism. Noncovalent interaction (NCI) analysis is a method to realize the visualization of the regions and types of weak interactions in a chemical system based on peaks appearing in reduced density gradient (RDG) at low electron density [48]. This analytical method has been used to study weak interactions on graphene systems [49]. Through a comprehensive analysis of the 3D diagram of the RDG isosurface (Fig. 6a1) and the 2D scatter diagram (Fig. 6a2), it can be seen that the initial graphite has layered characteristics and presents relatively regular electronic properties, showing two types of bonding, namely, the chemical bonding in the layer and the vdW interaction perpendicular to the plane structure. Specifically, the green isosurface in the RDG diagram clearly and intuitively shows the large area of pi-pi accumulation, reflecting the obvious vdW interaction region between the graphene. And the red isosurface clearly shows the steric resistance region in the center of each ring in the graphite system.

For the products irradiated by the single pulse femtosecond laser, the RDG isosurface is shown in Fig. 6b1 and Supplementary movie 1. In this case, it is observed that the green area between the top layer and the sub-surface layer decreases but still visible, indicating that the vdW interaction is weakened. In addition, the green area between the sub-surface layer and the third layer is also significantly reduced compared with the initial graphite, indicating that the single pulse has a higher energy density and can simultaneously excite electrons between different layers, so the single pulse is more conducive to multilayer exfoliating.

We use the same method to demonstrate the RDG isosurface (Fig. 6c1 and Supplementary movie 2) and the scatter diagram (Fig. 6c2) of the graphite system after double-pulse irradiation. It can be seen that the green isosurface between the top layer and the sub-surface layer of graphite in the final state structure of evolution can be ignored, while the green isosurface between the subsurface layer and the third layer, between the third layer and the innermost layer is still relatively complete, indicating that double pulses are more conducive to the formation of single layer QDs. This is consistent with the above analysis results about the structure. In order to clearly analyze the structural adjustment caused by double pulse irradiation, the scatter diagram of the irradiated graphite system was compared with the initial graphite structure. It was observed that new steep peaks appeared at the low value RDG pointing to the horizontal axis, indicating the increase of new weak interactions, which was consistent with the comparative analysis of the isosurface of RDG.

The following are the Supplementary data related to this article.
video.S1 video.S1
video. S2 video. S2

In order to further compare the irradiation effects of different laser types on the chemical bonds of the resulting graphite, the electronic local function (ELF) is also analyzed. ELF contains information about the probability of electron pairs and is an effective way to distinguish the properties of different types of bonds [50,51]. Fig. 6a3, 6b3, 6c3 shows the 2D cross-section of the top graphite. As can be clearly seen from Fig. 6a3, the initial structure shows a uniform charge density distribution, and there are highly localized electrons between the C–C atoms of the top graphite, which determines the covalent properties of the C–C bond. The ELF of the C–C bond of the graphite structure in Fig. 6b3 shows that after the single pulse laser ablation, the charge density between the C atoms becomes weaker, and the ELF value of the marked area in Fig. 6b3 is 0.85. Fig. 6c3 shows the ELF distribution of the top graphite in the graphite system under the irradiation of double pulse. It can be clearly seen that the ELF value between C atoms is about 0.63, indicating that the C–C bond is also weakened.

To verify the universal applicability of the above conclusions, we prepared antimony quantum dots using single pulse and double pulse femtosecond laser to ablate antimony bulk targets in aqueous solution. It can be clearly seen from the TEM and AFM images that the distribution of antimony quantum dots obtained by double pulse laser ablation is uniform. It can be seen that the size distribution range of antimony quantum dots is relatively narrow, ranging from 1 to 6 nm, and the average size is about 3.5 nm (Fig. S10). Moreover, its thickness distribution is relatively uniform, with an average thickness of about 1.4 nm, indicating that the antimonene quantum dots obtained by double pulse processing are mainly in its double-layer form (Fig. S11), which is significantly less than the thickness of the antimonene quantum dots obtained by the conventional preparation method [52]. Similarly, similar morphology analysis was performed on the products obtained under the ablation of single-pulse laser, and the results were shown in Figs. S12-13. It can be seen that the concentration of antimony quantum dots prepared by single-pulse laser is relatively low, and the size distribution range is wide, ranging from 1 to 11 nm. According to AFM analysis, the thickness of antimony quantum dots prepared under single pulse condition ranges from 1.4 to 3.3 nm, which indicates that there are bilayer and multilayer of antimony quantum dots in the sample. Such results are consistent with the above conclusion that the temporally shaped femtosecond laser can make the processing process more controllable and the preparation efficiency of quantum dots is higher.

In summary, by designing and optimizing the key parameters of laser beam, we transformed traditional Gaussian single pulse into ultrafast pulse sequences through spatiotemporal shaping, and realized the controllable and rapid preparation of monolayer GQDs. The photo exfoliating mechanism of the layered materials under the ablation of temporally shaped femtosecond laser and traditional Gaussian single-pulse are studied by the combination of TDDFT and MD. The results show that the temporally shaped femtosecond laser can achieve multi-level excitation of electrons in the topmost layer of graphite, induce positive charge preferentially to accumulate in the topmost graphite layer, and generate repulsion force between the outermost graphite layer, thus realizing the single-layer exfoliating of graphite. Therefore, by changing the energy distribution of ultrashort laser pulse in time, the electronic dynamics of the material can be significantly regulated to achieve the single layer exfoliating of 2D materials. And such conclusions are expected to be robust for the exfoliation of various zero-dimension QDs.
Data will be made available on request.

Junrui Wu: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Le Ma: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Lan Jiang: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Xin Li: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Shaoqian Wang: Data curation. Mengyao Tian: Formal analysis. Sumei Wang: Supervision. Pei Zuo: Methodology.
